Good morning. Tourist Information, how can I help you?
Good morning. The museum is sold out today, so we have a free morning and no plan. What is open now, and not too crowded?
Let me check. First: how much time do you have?
About three hours. And our budget is small, sixty euros for two people, and that is the whole day.
Then the canal boat. It leaves in twenty minutes, it is one hour, and it is eighteen euros each. There are only a few places left on that one.
Twenty minutes... All right, I will take two. Where do I get on?
Behind the station, at the water. And there is a guided tour of the old city at eleven, but that one is sold out today, I am afraid.
Sold out again! And tomorrow? What are the opening hours?
Tomorrow there are a lot of places. Eleven o'clock, and it is off-peak, so the streets are quiet. It is ten euros, and in my opinion it is worth it.
Then I will book tomorrow at eleven too. Thank you, you saved my morning.
It is sold out? All right. I will ask.
How much time do we have before it closes?
Then I will take two for tomorrow at nine, please.
What are the opening hours, and is it crowded at that time?
Let me check. I will look it up.

Good morning, and thank you for calling the Van Gogh Museum in Amsterdam. Please listen to this information before you come. First, our opening hours. We are open every day, from nine in the morning until six in the evening. On Friday we close at nine in the evening. Second, your ticket. We do not sell tickets at the door anymore. Every ticket is online, and every ticket has a time slot: the half hour when you come in. If you arrive at eleven, and your time slot is two, we cannot let you in. So please, look at your ticket. Third, today. Today is Saturday, and I am sorry: today is sold out. There are no tickets left for today, not one. But please listen, because there is good news. Tomorrow morning at nine we still have a lot of tickets. Nine in the morning is our off-peak time slot: there are not many people, the rooms are quiet, and the sunflowers are almost yours alone. At two in the afternoon this museum is very crowded, and you see a lot of heads and a little art. So come early. Fourth, the guided tour. The tour is one hour, it is nine euros, and there are only a few places on each tour, so book it together with your ticket. Is it worth it? Many of our visitors say yes: with the tour, you understand what you are looking at. And last: if you have a little free time before your slot, the garden and the shop are open to everybody, and they cost nothing. Thank you, and we hope to see you tomorrow morning.

Hello. My name is Saskia, and I am at the next table, with my coffee. Is this your first morning in Amsterdam? Can I tell you something? I am going to tell you the only thing I know about this city, and it is not in your guidebook. My husband and I come here twice a year. And for many years I had an itinerary. A real one, on paper, with a schedule for every hour. Nine o'clock, the museum. Eleven, the canal. One, lunch. Three, the market. It was beautiful, and it never worked. Never! Something is always sold out, or it rains, or there is a lot of traffic, and by eleven o'clock the whole day is broken, and I am angry, and my husband is very quiet. So now we do it differently. We plan two things. Only two! We are going to see one museum in the morning, and we are going to eat somewhere near the water in the evening. That is our itinerary. Two lines. And all the hours in the middle? Free time. That is where the day happens. Last October we had a few hours with no plan, and we walked past a little door with music behind it, and I said, oh, I will go in. Just like that. I decided it there, on the street, in one second. It was a small concert in a church, six euros, and it is the most beautiful thing I have ever done in this city. It was not in my schedule. It could not be. So here is my advice, from one traveler to another. Make a little plan, not a big one. Leave a lot of space in the middle. And when the day gives you something, say yes on the spot. It is worth it.
